verses.commentary: Jesus goes to his hometown (Nazareth). Return of the native. Disciples accompany him. They will witness rejection.
Sabbath teaching in the synagogue. Many amazed. Where did he get this wisdom? These miracles? Questions imply suspicion. They want credentials, not truth.

Isn't this the carpenter? Mary's son? We know his brothers and sisters. Familiarity breeds contempt. They cannot see beyond his origins. They took offense (eskandalizonto, ἐσκανδαλίζοντο). Stumbled over him.
A prophet is not without honor except in his hometown, among his relatives, in his own home. Jesus speaks a universal truth. Proximity obscures perception. Those closest often see least.

He could not do many miracles there. Stunning statement. Unbelief constrains even divine power. Not inability, but incompatibility. Miracles require faith. He heals a few, but the full display of power is withheld.

Jesus was amazed at their lack of faith. One of only two times Jesus is amazed (the other: the centurion's faith, Matthew 8:10). Unbelief in the presence of such evidence astounds even him. He moves on, teaching village to village.
Jesus sends the Twelve out two by two. Partnership. Mutual support. Authority over impure spirits delegated. Mission begins.
Take nothing: no bread, bag, money. Only a staff. Radical dependence. Trust in God's provision through people's hospitality.
Sandals yes, extra shirt no. Minimal provisions. Travel light. Urgency and trust combined.
Stay in one house until you leave. Don't trade up for better accommodations. Contentment. Loyalty to first host.
If any place rejects you, shake the dust off your feet. Symbolic act. Testimony against them. Judgment pronounced. Move on. Not all will receive the kingdom.
They preached repentance. Same message as John and Jesus. The call to turn from sin, turn to God.
They drove out demons, anointed with oil, healed. Jesus' ministry multiplied through them. The Twelve do what Jesus does. Oil as sign and sacrament.
King Herod hears of Jesus. Fame reaches the palace. Some say John the Baptist raised from the dead. Guilt produces wild speculation.
Others say Elijah or a prophet. Correct categories but wrong identification. They see prophet, miss Messiah.
Herod's verdict: John, whom I beheaded, has been raised. Conscience speaks. Past sin haunts him. He fears resurrection because he knows he killed a righteous man.
Flashback. Herod arrested John because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife, whom he married. Adultery and incest. Power compounds sin.
John confronted Herod: It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife. Prophetic courage. Truth to power. Leviticus 18:16, 20:21 violated.
Herodias nursed a grudge, wanted John dead. But she was unable. A woman's rage thwarted by her husband's fear.

Herod feared John, protected him, knowing him righteous and holy. He was puzzled by John but liked to listen. Fascination with righteousness he would not embrace. Moral paralysis: drawn to truth, enslaved to sin.
The opportune time came. Herod's birthday banquet. High officials, military commanders, leading men of Galilee. Stage set for public manipulation.
Herodias' daughter dances. Herod and guests are pleased. Foolish oath: Ask anything, I'll give it. Rash promise, public witness. Pride traps him.
Whatever you ask, up to half my kingdom. Echoes Esther 5:3, 7:2. Ironic parallel: Esther's request saved lives; this request will take one.
She asks her mother. What should I request? Herodias seizes the moment: The head of John the Baptist. Vengeance long planned now possible.
The girl hurries back: I want the head of John the Baptist on a platter, right now. Urgency. Gruesome specificity. Youth corrupted by maternal hatred.

Herod is greatly distressed. Conflict between conscience and pride. But because of his oaths and dinner guests, he cannot refuse. Public honor valued above private conscience. Reputation trumps righteousness.
He sends an executioner immediately. John beheaded in prison. Swift injustice. The greatest prophet meets a violent end.
Head brought on a platter, presented to the girl, given to her mother. Grotesque trophy. Evil celebrates.
John's disciples take his body, lay it in a tomb. Faithful to the end. Honor the prophet. The forerunner's death prefigures Jesus' own.
The apostles (first use of this term in Mark) gather around Jesus, report what they did and taught. Debriefing. Mission trip ends; they return to Jesus.
Jesus says, Come with me to a quiet place and rest. Pastoral care. Ministry is draining. So many people coming and going they couldn't eat. Rest is not optional.
They go by boat to a solitary place. Seeking withdrawal. Sabbath for the soul.
But people recognize them, run on foot, arrive ahead of them. No escape. Need pursues. Rest deferred.

Jesus sees the crowd, has compassion. They are like sheep without a shepherd (Numbers 27:17, 1 Kings 22:17, Ezekiel 34:5). Scattered, vulnerable, leaderless. He begins teaching. Compassion leads to instruction. The true shepherd feeds his flock with truth.
Late in the day. Disciples practical: This is remote, it's late.
Send them away to buy food. Reasonable solution. They focus on logistics.
Jesus: You give them something to eat. Impossible command. They calculate: More than half a year's wages needed. Are we to spend that much? They think money; Jesus thinks miracle.
How many loaves do you have? Go and see. Jesus redirects them from what they lack to what they have. Five loaves and two fish. Inadequate resources, but sufficient for God.
Have them sit in groups on green grass. Order. Green grass suggests spring (Passover season). Echoes Psalm 23: He makes me lie down in green pastures.
They sit in groups of hundreds and fifties. Organized. Echoes Exodus 18:21 (Moses' arrangement of Israel). The new exodus requires new structure.

Jesus takes the loaves and fish, looks to heaven, gives thanks, breaks, gives to disciples to distribute. Liturgical actions. Host of the meal. Anticipates the Last Supper. God is the source; Jesus is the mediator; disciples are the distributors.
Abundance, not scarcity. Hunger fully met. Complete satisfaction.
Twelve baskets of leftovers. More after the meal than before. One basket per apostle. Multiplication beyond need. God's provision exceeds expectation.
Five thousand men. Women and children not counted; total crowd likely ten to fifteen thousand. Massive miracle. Echoes Elisha feeding 100 (2 Kings 4:42–44), but exponentially greater.
Immediately Jesus makes disciples get into the boat. Compels them. They need separation from the crowd. He dismisses the crowd. Authority to gather and to disperse.
He goes up a mountain to pray. After intense ministry, he seeks the Father. Communion sustains mission.
Night falls. Boat in the middle of the lake. Jesus alone on land. Physical separation.

He sees them straining at the oars. Wind against them. He perceives their struggle from a distance. Before dawn, he walks on the lake. Divine action. Job 9:8, Psalm 77:19: God treads on the waves. He was about to pass by them. Echoes God passing by Moses (Exodus 33:19–23), Elijah (1 Kings 19:11). Theophany.
They see him walking on water, think he's a ghost. Terror. Supernatural appearance interpreted as threat.
They all saw him, were terrified. Universal fear. Jesus speaks: Take courage! It is I (Ego eimi, Ἐγώ εἰμι). Divine name (Exodus 3:14). Don't be afraid. Reassurance.
He climbs into the boat, the wind dies. Presence brings peace. They are amazed. Still don't understand.

They had not understood about the loaves; their hearts were hardened. Stunning indictment. They saw the multiplication but missed the meaning. Hard hearts resist revelation. Spiritual blindness despite proximity to Jesus.
They land at Gennesaret. New location, same pattern.
People recognize Jesus immediately. Fame precedes him.
They run throughout the region, carry the sick on mats to wherever he is. Desperate faith mobilizes.

Villages, towns, countryside. Everywhere he goes, they bring the sick. They beg to touch the edge of his cloak. All who touched were healed. Mass healing. The kingdom floods the land. Chapter six: rejection at home, mission of the Twelve, John's martyrdom, feeding of thousands, walking on water. Power and peril intertwine.
